text.title: requirements-validation Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for requirements-validation. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Distinguish validation ("are we building the right thing?") from verification ("are we building it right?"); never run a verification checklist and call it validation.
rule.rationale: Verification checks form, ambiguity, and testability; validation checks business value. Conflating them means value is never confirmed, only syntax is reviewed.

rule.statement: Run a verification pass first (form, ambiguity, testability), then run the validation session; do not mix them in a single review.
rule.rationale: When validation sessions debate typos and ambiguities, stakeholders exhaust their time and attention before value questions are reached.

rule.statement: Check all eight quality attributes during verification before any validation session begins.
rule.rationale: Presenting unverified requirements for validation wastes stakeholder time on fixable defects that should have been caught earlier.

rule.statement: Cap validation sessions at 25 requirements and 90 minutes; run multiple passes rather than one marathon.
rule.rationale: Longer sessions produce rubber-stamp sign-offs as stakeholder attention exhausts; split sessions surface more issues per requirement reviewed.

rule.statement: Select the validation technique by requirement type, not by familiarity; a single technique misses what another would expose.
rule.rationale: Walkthrough alone misses what a prototype review or simulation would expose — BABOK enumerates 11+ validation techniques; each is optimized for a different requirement type.

rule.statement: Anchor every validation pass to the Business Need and Solution Scope produced in BABOK 5.1/6.1; do not validate requirements without a stated value model.
rule.rationale: Without a value model (KPIs, OKRs, benefit map), validation devolves into stakeholder opinion about what sounds reasonable.

rule.statement: Use adversarial framing in validation prompts: "list three ways each requirement could fail to deliver value" before scoring; avoid "are these requirements OK?"
rule.rationale: LLMs trend toward yes when asked for approval; adversarial framing surfaces defects that affirmative-framing suppresses.
